text.title: Product Lifecycle Management Core Rules
text.summary: Four-stage diagnostic (Introduction -> Growth -> Maturity -> Decline) matching stage signals to investment strategy, with explicit transition checkpoints and end-of-life sunset plan.

rule.statement: Stage tag MUST be derived from quantitative signals: growth rate, churn, NPS, market share. Qualitative 'feels mature' tagging is forbidden.

rationale.source: BCG Growth-Share Matrix; Boston Consulting lifecycle research

rule.rationale: Vibes-based stage tagging hides decline and over-invests in maturity.

rule.statement: Investment strategy MUST match the tagged stage: Introduction = learn fast, Growth = scale, Maturity = retain + efficiency, Decline = harvest or sunset.
rule.rationale: Growth budgets on Maturity products produce no acceleration.

rule.statement: Stage transitions MUST be marked with a written transition memo ('why we believe X -> Y now').
rule.rationale: Without checkpoints, teams stay in the previous stage's strategy long after the metrics moved.

rule.statement: Tagging a product Decline MUST trigger a sunset plan within 30 days: migration timeline, customer comms, EOL date, data-export commitments.
rule.rationale: Open-ended Decline products drain budget and frustrate customers.

rule.statement: Every product MUST be lifecycle-tagged BEFORE roadmap sequencing for the year. Tagging after the roadmap is a rationalization.
rule.rationale: Post-hoc tagging is reverse-engineered to justify the roadmap.

rule.statement: If applicability conditions fail (any Skip-If trigger holds — single-product team; pre-PMF; agency projects; recent tag without trigger events), the agent MUST skip this methodology and route to the named alternative or to the methodology fallback.
rule.rationale: Mis-applying a methodology outside its preconditions degrades output quality and pollutes downstream artefacts; the explicit skip-rule keeps the routing tree honest.
